topic: Two-pole geomagnetic model — toroidal architecture with B(r) equation, flux conservation failure (change-driven re-review)
change_summary: Since c2 (2026-04-07): detail_evidence grew 1197→3872 chars (+223%), detail_verdict_text grew 238→2061 chars (+766%). New material includes (a) aperture sensitivity analysis at 1000 km (415× discrepancy), (b) explicit refutation of dome-wins.html ‘zero field at geographic South Pole’ strawman, (c) standard dipole calculation at θ≈170°, (d) post-hoc trivial-transcription argument added to detail_verdict_text, (e) PRED-TOROID-002 retrodiction note (confirmation date 2026-03-23 precedes registration 2026-04-04).
our_argument_summary: WIN-053: Dome claims Closed Toroidal Ovoid geometry produces dipole-like field via sub-terrestrial return path. Verdict Self-Contradicted. Core argument: flux conservation within the dome’s own geometry requires B_south ≈ B_north/1600 ≈ 39 nT for a 500 km pole aperture (or B_south ≈ 156 nT for a 1000 km aperture — still ~415× short of the fitted 64,852 nT). Secondary arguments: (1) Griffiths §5.3.3 1/r form contradicts exponential decay; (2) coefficients 62,376 and 64,852 nT fit observed pole strengths (~60 k / ~66 k nT per WMM2025) rather than derived from dome parameters; (3) 20% RMS ≈ 10–13 kT, ~200× worse than IGRF-13; (4) the shared scale-length λ = 8619 km is reused for both H(r) and B(r) with no independent derivation; (5) the refactor introduced a self-contradiction absent from V50.6; (6) PRED-TOROID-002 registered 2026-04-04 but ‘confirmed’ 2026-03-23 — retrodiction.

raw-text/05-model.txt (V50.6 — no toroidal model, only monopolar vortex)

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No monitoring for WIN-053. monitor.py retains 39 V50.6 domains; no new domain was added for WIN-053 when V51.0 introduced it. The B(r) equation and its claimed 20% RMS improvement are static website prose, not computed from a live fetch.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No monitoring exists. Static text only.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Two fitted coefficients (62,376 and 64,852 nT) and a shared scale λ = 8,619 km can be retuned to any observed dipole. The aetheric refractive index n(r) sits in reserve for any positional/angular discrepancies.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. Review correctly notes no monitoring exists for WIN-053.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: V50.6 raw text (02-wins.txt, 05-model.txt) lists only 39 WINs and no toroidal architecture; V51.0 added WIN-053 without extending monitor.py. No INTERMAGNET/CHAOS-7/Swarm fetch validates B(r).
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Earth dipole field is described by IGRF-13 (168 spherical harmonic coefficients) and CHAOS-7 with ~50 nT RMS. Dome relabels the geodynamo as aetheric toroidal circulation but predicts nothing these models don’t already predict with ~200× better accuracy.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: B(r) coefficients ≈ observed pole strengths (62,376≈58–60 k nT north; 64,852≈65–67 k nT south per WMM2025). Pole strengths have been measured continuously since Gauss (1838).
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: Only λ = 8,619 km connects to a dome parameter (shared with H(r)). The two critical coefficients 62,376 and 64,852 have no geometric derivation — they are fitted. The exponential form itself is incompatible with toroidal geometry (which per Griffiths §5.3.3 gives 1/r inside an ideal winding; see hole #2 for nuance).

holes_found.description: ATTRIBUTION ERROR: "standard centered dipole gives ~54,000 nT at the geographic south pole." A pure centered-dipole calculation with Earth’s dipole moment m ≈ 7.94×10^22 A·m² and R = 6,371 km gives B_eq = μ₀m/(4πR³) ≈ 30,700 nT, hence B(θ≈170°) ≈ 30,700 × √(1+3·cos²170°) ≈ 60,700 nT. The ~54,000–55,000 nT figure the review cites is actually the IGRF-13 measured/modeled value at the geographic south pole, which is SMALLER than pure dipole because higher-order multipoles subtract at that latitude. Attributing the IGRF value to the pure centered dipole is a physics error. A reader (or a dome defender) who re-runs the pure-dipole formula will find ~60 kT, not 54 kT, and can accuse us of not understanding the physics we invoke. The same paragraph then cites IGRF-13 at ~55,000 nT, which compounds the confusion: the text reads as though pure dipole and IGRF-13 produce roughly the same number, when in fact they differ by ~10%. This does not change the refutation of the dome’s ‘zero field’ strawman (which only requires a non-zero value), but the specific number and its attribution are wrong.
holes_found.recommendation: Fix the attribution. Example rewrite: ‘A pure centered-dipole calculation with Earth’s dipole moment gives B(θ≈170°) ≈ 60,700 nT, while IGRF-13 — which includes higher-order multipoles — reports ~55,000 nT at the geographic south pole. Either way, the globe predicts a STRONG field there, not zero.’ This is both factually correct and makes the refutation of the ‘zero field’ strawman stronger, not weaker.

holes_found.description: OVER-SPECIFIC 1/r CLAIM VS GRIFFITHS §5.3.3. Griffiths §5.3.3 (Example 5.12) gives B = μ₀NI/(2πs) inside a tightly wound toroidal coil — a very specific geometry (dense uniform winding around a donut). The dome’s ‘Closed Toroidal Ovoid’ is not a wound solenoid; it is a single aetheric flow from an axial source, across a disc surface, through a single sub-terrestrial return. The actual field inside such a large single-loop circuit is geometry-dependent (Biot–Savart integral over the whole current distribution) and is NOT simply 1/r. A physics-trained dome defender can legitimately push back: ‘Griffiths §5.3.3 applies to toroidal coils, not to your characterization of our architecture.’ The FLUX CONSERVATION argument (independent of any particular field form) is airtight and does all the work; the 1/r appeal is rhetorical filler that makes us look overreaching.
holes_found.recommendation: Reframe so flux conservation stands alone and the 1/r point is presented as a subordinate observation, not as the Griffiths-backed claim that the dome’s exponential ‘contradicts standard EM.’ Example: ‘Whatever the field geometry, ∇·B = 0 alone requires B_return × A_return = B_source × A_source. Given the dome’s ~1,600:1 area asymmetry, B_south cannot exceed B_north / ~1,600. (Separately, real toroidal coils produce 1/r interior fields per Griffiths §5.3.3; the dome’s exponential matches no standard closed-circuit geometry, but the flux-conservation bound binds regardless of functional form.)’

holes_found.description: THE DOME-SIDE ‘ZERO FIELD’ QUOTE IS PARAPHRASED, NOT SOURCED. Our text asserts: ‘The dome’s wins.html claims globe dipole theory predicts zero field at geographic South Pole.’ The raw-text/ archive available in this clone contains the V50.6 dump only; the V51.0 wins.html text for WIN-053 is not captured there, and no URL or direct quote is given. If the dome site does not literally say ‘zero field,’ we are strawmanning the dome author — which is exactly the failure mode we criticize the dome for. If the dome site does say it (even approximately), we should quote the verbatim phrase and link to the anchor.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) add a direct verbatim quote with anchor/URL (e.g., ‘https://john09289.github.io/predictions/wins.html#WIN-053’) plus an archive.org snapshot, or (b) downgrade the sentence to characterize what the dome argues rather than what it ‘claims,’ e.g., ‘The dome frames the globe dipole as unable to explain a strong southern pole field.’ Until this is sourced, Cycle 3 recommends (b) as the safer fix.

holes_found.description: APERTURE VALUE STILL UNTETHERED TO A DOME PARAMETER. The 500 km ‘pole aperture’ is described as ‘estimated from model geometry,’ but the dome site does not publish an Axis Mundi source radius. The cycle-2 recommendation asked for an explicit sensitivity note; the current text DOES include a 1,000 km check (400:1 → 415× short). That is the right structural fix. What is still missing is an upper-bound argument: even at the most generous aperture the dome could conceivably adopt (say, the Arctic Circle r ≈ 2,606 km, or the north pole ‘mountain’ radius), the ratio stays ≥ 60:1 and B_south still has to be ≤ ~1,000 nT — still ~60× short. Adding one more row to the sensitivity analysis closes the ‘but what if the aperture is huge’ quibble permanently.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one more sensitivity point, e.g., ‘Even at a 3,000 km aperture — far larger than anything the dome describes for the Axis Mundi — the ratio is still ~45:1, bounding B_south ≤ ~1,400 nT, ~46× below the fitted value.’

holes_found.description: DIRECTION OF FLUX NOT DISCUSSED. Flux conservation constrains both magnitude and direction. In the dome’s claimed toroidal circuit, the north pole is a SOURCE (field lines emerging vertically from Axis Mundi) and the south/return is a SINK (field lines converging into the Sump). The fitted B(r) gives two similar-magnitude north-max / south-max peaks, but does not specify directionality; in the dome’s own cartoon, both poles are at the surface of the disc — i.e., both are ‘source-like’ with respect to the observed surface field. A true closed circuit cannot have two co-directional maxima on the same side of the return. This is a second internal contradiction beyond the magnitude mismatch: the fitted equation does not describe a closed loop at all.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional addition to detail_evidence: one sentence noting that flux conservation additionally requires the two peaks to be oppositely directed with respect to the return surface, which the dome’s B(r) does not enforce.

holes_found.description: MISSING CROSS-REFERENCE TO SECTION 3.5.8 (still open from cycle 2). The cycle-2 review flagged this and the recommendation was not applied. Section 3.5.8 (‘WIN-012 single-pole collapse’) overlaps substantially with the WIN-053 argument about fitted coefficients and the shared λ. Readers landing on either independently miss the other.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a one-line cross-reference in detail_evidence or detail_extra: ‘See §3.5.8 for the parallel single-pole collapse affecting WIN-012 and the κ coupling constant.’

stronger_arguments.description: SURFACE-VS-SUBSURFACE CLARIFICATION PREEMPTS THE STRONGEST DEFENSE. A dome defender could argue that the 64,852 nT at ‘south’ refers to the sub-terrestrial return path (the ‘Sump’ near the Antarctic ice wall), not a surface field. Preempt this: the dome’s claimed 20% RMS improvement is computed against surface observatories (INTERMAGNET / CHAOS-7 validation stations, which sit on the disc surface). If the 64,852 nT were sub-terranean, there would be no way to validate it against the data the dome itself uses. Adding ‘both the dome’s fit and the IGRF comparison use surface-observatory data; the 64,852 nT is a surface prediction, not a subsurface value’ closes this escape hatch.

THE λ = 8,619 km DOUBLE-USE IS A SECOND GLOBE FINGERPRINT. λ = 8,619 km is fit from H(r), derived from a quadrature of Polaris-elevation observations through the very aetheric medium whose refractive index is claimed to bend light. If n(r) is strong enough to shift the sun’s apparent altitude (Section 1.8), the Polaris data used to fit H(r) is also contaminated — and therefore so is λ, and so is the B(r) fit. The ‘zero fitted parameters’ claim is doubly false: λ itself is fitted, and it is fitted to a contaminated dataset.

RETRODICTION TIMELINE SHOULD BE ARCHIVED. The current text states: ‘PRED-TOROID-002 registered 2026-04-04 but ‘confirmation’ date 2026-03-23 precedes registration.’ This is a concrete, checkable timestamp claim. If we have not already archived the predictions.html page at both dates (the 2026-03-23 page lacking PRED-TOROID-002, the 2026-04-04 page containing it), an Internet Archive / archive.today capture on or near today’s date (2026-04-18) is the cheapest possible durable proof that the registration was post-hoc. A single archive.org URL footnote preempts any future deletion or silent backdating.
sources: Poller change logs in monitor/changes/ may already contain the first observation date of PRED-TOROID-002

kernel_of_truth.description: The toroidal architecture is a real improvement over V50.6’s monopolar vortex. A ring-magnet / closed-circuit topology is the correct direction for anyone wanting a dipole-like field, and recognizing ∇·B = 0 at all is more than many heterodox cosmologies do. Introducing a shared scale length λ across H(r) and B(r) is a parsimony move that an honest physicist would at least attempt. The effort to quantify the improvement via RMS (61% → 20%) engages with empirical validation rather than hand-waving.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The execution fails by the dome’s own rules. Flux conservation — the same principle that motivates a closed circuit — forbids the fitted equation: the north aperture and the return area differ by a factor of ~1,600, so the returning field must be ~1,600× smaller than the source unless the topology is open. The exponential form fits no standard closed-circuit geometry; the coefficients 62,376 and 64,852 match observed WMM2025 pole strengths without derivation; the 20% RMS is ~200× worse than IGRF-13 which uses a century of geodynamo physics. The architectural improvement was never mathematically worked through — it was adopted for qualitative plausibility, then fitted to the measurements it was supposed to predict.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A charitable dome defender would argue the 64,852 nT at the ‘southern pole’ term refers to the sub-terrestrial return flux density in the Sump, not the surface field. ‘Flux conservation is preserved because the return flow is in a narrower sub-terrestrial channel whose cross-section is much smaller than the full disc area. The 1,600:1 area ratio used by the reviewer assumes the return spans the entire disc — in fact the return is a constrained sub-crustal path with area closer to the polar aperture itself, preserving flux.’ They would add: ‘The exponential decay describes how surface observers see the aetheric field as distance from their local pole grows; the B(r) equation is an observer formula, not a claim about the sub-terrestrial return.’
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Add one paragraph to detail_evidence that closes this escape hatch explicitly: ‘The dome computes its 20% RMS improvement against surface-observatory measurements (INTERMAGNET/CHAOS-7 validation stations), so the 64,852 nT at the southern pole is being proposed as a SURFACE value. Reinterpreting it as a sub-terrestrial value would disconnect the equation from the data the dome itself uses to validate it. Moreover, if the return flux is channelized to a narrow sub-crustal path, the dome owes us both the channel cross-section and a mechanism by which a narrow sub-crustal flux of ~65 kT produces the ~55 kT measured at the surface through thousands of kilometres of rock — which is standard globe-physics territory.’

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: No cross-WIN contradictions in our review. WIN-053’s criticism of the fitted coefficients reinforces WIN-012 (κ = 1.67 nT/μGal derived using the same λ), and our WIN-013/WIN-014 eclipse-null arguments are consistent with the claim that the κ coupling constant has no operational support. WIN-037’s field-decay argument is consistent with treating pole strengths as observed rather than predicted. WIN-058b’s Bedrock Induction Ratio κ = 1.677 uses the same λ = 8,619 km machinery; if that machinery is unjustified here (WIN-053), it is unjustified there too — and our review says so in both places. A minor polish: ensure WIN-053 detail_extra mentions WIN-058b so readers follow the thread.

claims_checked: Disc area ≈ 1.26×10^9 km² at r=20,015 km
Standard centered dipole B(θ=170°) with Earth parameters
20% RMS ≈ 10–13 kT relative to ~50–60 kT typical field
quantitative_verification.computations: Python (math module). Area ratio at 500 km: π·500² / π·20015² ⇒ 1602.4, matching text’s 1,600:1. B_south at 500 km: 62,376 / 1602 = 38.9 nT, matching 39 nT. Discrepancy: 64,852 / 38.9 = 1,666× (text rounds to 1,660:1 — fine). 1,000 km aperture: 62,376 / 400.6 = 155.7 nT (text says 156 — fine); discrepancy 64,852 / 155.7 = 416.5× (text says 415× — fine). Standard dipole: B_eq = μ₀·(7.94×10²²)/(4π·(6.371×10⁶)³) = 30,704 nT; at θ=170° factor = √(1+3·cos²170°) = 1.977, giving B = 60,710 nT. TEXT SAYS 54,000 nT which DOES NOT MATCH the centered dipole it attributes the value to; 54–55 kT is the IGRF value at geographic south pole (multipolar corrections lower the dipole value by ~10%).
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: Standard-centered-dipole value at geographic south pole: review claims ~54,000 nT, actual pure-dipole calculation gives ~60,700 nT. The 54,000 nT figure is the IGRF-13 observed value (with higher-order multipoles). See hole #1 for recommended rewrite.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 3 assessment: The text has been substantially strengthened since cycle 2. The 1,000 km aperture sensitivity check (cycle-2 hole #1) has been applied correctly. The retrodiction timeline (PRED-TOROID-002 confirmed 2026-03-23, registered 2026-04-04) is a strong concrete addition. The post-hoc ‘trivial-transcription test’ paragraph in detail_verdict_text aligns the WIN with the Part 3.1b framework and makes the methodology coherent across WINs.

Three moderate holes remain:
(1) The ‘~54,000 nT standard centered dipole’ attribution is numerically wrong — pure dipole gives ~60,700 nT at θ=170°; the 54,000 nT figure is IGRF-13 observed. This is fixable with a one-sentence rewrite and actually STRENGTHENS our refutation of the dome’s ‘zero field’ framing.
(2) The 1/r ‘Griffiths §5.3.3’ claim overreaches: §5.3.3 describes a tightly wound toroidal coil, not a spread-out single-loop flow circuit. Flux conservation alone carries the argument; the 1/r appeal should be demoted to a subordinate aside.
(3) The dome-side ‘zero field at geographic south pole’ quote has no verified source. Either a direct quote with an archived URL or a softer framing is required.

Two minor holes: aperture sensitivity could go to 3,000 km for completeness; flux direction (source vs sink) is not discussed in detail_evidence.

The cross-reference to §3.5.8 flagged in cycle 2 has still not been applied.

Advocate-mode lens identifies the ‘sub-terrestrial return’ defense as the strongest rebuttal (rated 3/5) and recommends a preemptive paragraph.

Cross-WIN: consistent with WIN-012, WIN-013, WIN-014, WIN-037, WIN-058b. No inconsistencies.

Quantitative: all arithmetic verified except the standard-dipole value (hole #1). Recommended action is still minor_edit — the argument is sound; the fixes are tightening, not rewriting.

citations_verified: South Atlantic Anomaly EPSL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.epsl.2018.12.019 — DOI resolves to Finlay et al. / SAA paper; valid
citations_failed: Dome-side quote ‘globe dipole theory predicts zero field at geographic South Pole’ attributed to wins.html — no URL, anchor, or archive link provided; not verifiable from the V50.6 raw-text dump (which only captures 39 WINs, not WIN-053).
citations_unchecked: Griffiths, Introduction to Electrodynamics, §5.3.3 — textbook citation; formula is correct for a tightly wound toroidal coil but application to the dome’s geometry is over-broad (see hole #2).
Reported 61% → 20% RMS improvement — originates on the dome site; not independently recomputed. Our text presents it as the dome’s claim.
